sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE].
There's good. ?]
Lilly Wolf at present.
Born--
L-I-double L-Y and Wolf, W-O-L-F.
Yes.
I was Baruch, B-A-R-U-C-H.
And my birth-- my name on my birth certificate was Livia Anna.
L-I-V-I-A, and Anna, A-double N-A.
Yes.
I was married once in Hungary.
And my husband's name was Weinreb-- W-E-I-N-R-E-B.
And then we changed it to Varadi, Hungarianized.
V-A-R-A-D-I.
But we came out again to Australia.
I can't even remember, was it Varadi or Weinreb, one of the two.
I think we changed it back to Weinreb--
--because it was easier to pronounce it in English.
As a child I had a lot of freckles.
And they called me in school, Szepi.
S-Z-E-P-I.
Freckleface.
Freckly.
[LAUGHS]
I had, but I can't remember.
Don't know it.
Don't know it.
I was born on the 28th of the 7th.
And now you get a shock-- 1927.
The city was Budapest.
And the country was Hungary.
Well, my parents, my brother, and myself.
My father was Otto Baruch.
And my mother was Margaret [?
Muncie. ?] And her maiden name was Weiszenstein.
Yes, I had a brother who was born 14 months after me.
Fredi-- Alfred, Alfred Joseph Baruch.
And he perished, unfortunately, in the years of '45, January.
Yeah.
Yeah, well actually I had a very hectic early age.
My parents, my father, was a manager for a shoe manufacturers in Czechoslovakia, which was called [INAUDIBLE].
And his job was to go to North Africa and establish a shop, rent a-- a premises, furnish it, run it in, employ people to do it.
So that when-- he-- after he left, that was a going concern.
Now we did that for three years-- one year in Algier, one year in Oran, and Tangier.
And I was conceived in Africa.
And then my mother wanted me to be born and have Hungarian citizenship, so we came back to Hungary.
She gave birth to me.
And then after six weeks, I-- we went back to Budapest.
And there, she conceived my brother.
And again, she came back to Hungary, gave birth to Fredi.
With me-- I was with her-- and we went back to--
Africa.
And we were there until I was about three and 1/2, four years old.
Then we came back.
So actually, my mother tongue is French.
Because they spoke French in North Africa.
And on the way back-- we had to leave Africa, because my brother didn't-- the, the weather, the, the climate, didn't agree with him, and the food what was there.
So we came back all together.
My father gave up that particular job for the firm.
And they have given him a shop in-- well, actually a whole building-- and the bottom part, shop part-- in Wunstorf, which was Sudeten, Germany.
There, we lived until-- that was a very happy part of my life-- we lived until-- I went to school there till I was about eight years old.
Germany after Africa.
I must have been around about four years old, something like that.
Because I think we spent some time in Budapest with the family and so on.
And I must have been about four years old, something like that.
I can't remember exactly.
But in Wunstorf, where we lived, that was, as I said, very, very pleasant area.
And we stayed there until I was 10-- nine, nine.
And then for a year, I had to live with my grandmother in Bratislava, [INAUDIBLE], which is a border--
Yes.
I went to a German language school.
And it's funny that those first two years or three years of schooling in-- two years of schooling-- in Wunstorf, I still cannot say the ABC any other way but in the German.
Well, it's been brainwashed into my mind.
And I can't say it any other way.
Counting is different-- counting, I do it in Hungarian or in English.
But the ABC goes to me automatically in German.
That's right, in Wunstorf.
Funny, I don't remember anything of school time in Wunstorf.
It's more our private life, which was very pleasant.
I did not feel any anti-Semitism.
I only know that we belonged to a certain middle-class crowd, where my brother and I were always dressed the same way.
We had the same Austrian uniform.
Or we had the same Scottish uniform, she in a, he in the little pants and I in a skirt.
But the hat were the same and the blouses.
And I've still got photos where we, both of us, were photographed in the same sort of outfit.
And I can, I can remember we were once in a teddy bear outfit.
You know, these are the little memories which are coming back to me.
I can see myself.
And I looked at my brother.
And I thought, isn't he cute.
[LAUGHTER]
Yeah, that-- those years were very, very pleasant.
School years, I don't remember at all.
I don't remember a teacher.
I do know we had a nanny, my brother and I.
And in the beginning I didn't like her much.
She was too authoritarian.
And I rebelled against it.
Yes, that was all in Wunstorf.
But eventually, I think I [LAUGHS] mellowed down to her and did what she was telling us.
Stefi.
Yeah, she was very nice lady.
I have photos of her, too, funny enough, after so many years.
Anyway, these were nice times in Wunstorf.
And then, as I said, that was already close to '38-- '37, '37, '36.
And I had to go over for one year to my grandma, my paternal grandmother.
They lived in Bratislava, in [INAUDIBLE], with her daughter and her son-in-law.
And I lived there for a year, which was very pleasant as well.
But I didn't have none of my parents or my brother with me.
I was alone with my grandma sort of thing.
I can't remember exactly.
I think my mother came back to Hungary with my brother, who wasn't-- he was healthy by that time.
But he was always somehow physically the weaker one out of the two.

And now with common sense, I think that the birth, after 14 months of the first child, me, who was a very healthy, strong girl, he suffered by my mother's not giving herself enough time between one and the second child.
And she came back to Budapest.
And I stayed with Grandma.
Why, what was the background, I don't really know, never questioned it.
And I didn't see my father much after that, because after Bratislava, I came to Budapest.
Yes.
None of the schools, again.
I don't know, is that a [LAUGHS] block, a mental block against schools?
On the other hand, I remember when we got-- when I got back to Budapest, I couldn't speak any Hungarian at all.
They made me, more or less, forget French.
Because I had to concentrate on German to be good in school in Wunstorf.
So automatically, my French was totally neglected.
And when I came back to Budapest, they put me in a boarding school, where I was sort of transformed from German into Hungarian language.
About nine, nine and 1/2, something like that.
Now, I can't remember was I there for a year, or nine months, or 15 months?
That, I can't remember.
Yes, but, but I was in the boarding school, live-in.
So I wasn't reunited as a family.
I, I most probably-- that, I can't remember, either-- I was visited on the weekend.
Or they picked me up for the weekend.
It was in Budapest, in the city, so it wasn't-- it shouldn't have been any hardship to pick me up or to visit me.
But I can't recall any of it.
No.
None, none whatsoever.

No, somehow I was never conscious-- only later in years-- I was never conscious of anti-Semitic remarks, only later on in schools, but not in the beginning, not-- neither in Wunstorf nor in the boarding school.
After the boarding school, with my brother together, we had to go to a country boarding school.
Because what happened was that my grandmother, my maternal grandmother, has given us a home in her apartment with my auntie together, my mother's sister.
And she hadn't-- didn't have enough room for all of us.
So my brother Fredi and I went into the country to another boarding school.
Believe it or not, it was a nunnery.
So I was staying in the girl's department.
And my brother was staying in the priestly department.
And we, we were there for about a year and a half to two years.
Oh yes, oh yes, very much aware, so much so that when Christmas came, all the children went with their parents but us.
Fredi and I were staying in the school, in the boarding school.
And there, I have some pleasant and unpleasant memories.
So by then, I was fully conscious of what was going on.
And we had some remarks thrown at us that we were Jews.
So I was about-- '37, '38, '39, something like that.
Oh, yeah-- not students, teachers.
The-- well, being a nunnery, I don't know, and I can't recall, any strong derogatory remarks.
But there was a difference between a Jewish girl and a non-Jewish girl, if you know what I mean.
They have made a remark, or they called you names, which weren't over-nasty, but they made a difference whether you are Jewish or not Jewish.
Oh, yes, I was aware.
And I was aware that, that we were somehow second-rate citizens, if not a third.
[SIGHS] Look, I was 10, 11 years old, I wasn't very conscious of it.
I can't remember.
I know that my parents and grandmother had problems.
And I overheard certain worries they had, and remarks, and so on.
But it was never somehow discussed with us.
Somehow, they wanted to protect us.
Or they think-- they thought that we couldn't give any positive suggestions what to do and how to do.
So we weren't involved in any discussions.
But of course, we heard that they were listening to the radios, or the war, where, what, and how.
Well, not in Hungary, in the world.
Well, I tell you what we felt, that the Jews were losing slowly their, their academic position, that kids couldn't go to university.
Of course, numerus clausus and numerus nullus came after, after that.
But that Jews could not keep the position in their firm.
Let's say they had a big factory.
It was taken away from them nice and slowly.
We, we were very liberal.
I must agree that we didn't keep a Friday night.
We didn't observe it at all.
On the other hand, both my brothers and my became bar mitzvah and bat mitzvah.
And we--
[INAUDIBLE] No, both my brother and I became bar mitzvah and bat mitzvah.

But it was more so a pushing of my uncle-- a great-uncle, my grandmother's cousin, I think, twice removed-- who said that he definitely-- he was a religious man and had a kosher household, and financially, very nicely liquid.
So he supported wherever he could.
And he pushed his foot down that the two of us have to have our bat mitzvah and bar mitzvah.
Otherwise, we only had the two to three high holy days in the year, which as you know, the Pesach, and Rosh Hashanah, and Yom Kippur was always there.
As a matter of fact-- well, I will tell you after.
The Yom Kippur, I had a very nasty experience.
After the war, they were-- I kept the Yom Kippur.
And I collapsed in the toilet about 3:00 o'clock in the afternoon.
And I was forever and ever after taken off-- I'm not allowed to diet, meaning fast.
Oh, yes, oh, yes, high holy days we always kept.
But we didn't have the Friday night rituals, or the Saturday morning going to synagogue, or anything like that.
We did pick up the telephone.
And in Budapest, there were mixed and majority very [? neoliberal ?] people, where there are-- there were quite a few who were very religious and had a kosher household.
But these things were more in the country.
The city, big city, people were more liberal.
They had the few synagogues where they went to.
There were yeshivas in our area as well, because we lived in a, let's say, the equivalent of Bondi here.
It was a Jewish area, but it wasn't a typical-- the street where we lived, it wasn't a Jewish street.
It's only behind it was a Jewish area.
So if we wanted to, we did go to synagogues.
But we, as I said, us ourselves were not religious in that respect.
Yes, but there I don't remember having a Pesach or a Yom Kippur.
In Wunstorf.
Now, I don't know whether was that on purpose, or my parents.
I haven't got any idea.
Whether they were afraid that-- of course by that time Hitler existed.
And seeing that it was a Sudeten German township, they didn't want to rock the boat and tell everybody that we're Jewish, I don't know.
I really don't know, I mean.
That, I don't know.
Not in Wunstorf-- in Budapest we were already with Jewish friends, yes.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
I remember that when we came back from that country boarding school into Budapest, we had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a housekeeper who was, naturally, Christian.
And she was very, very decent, our homekeeper.
But in the block of units, there was a caretaker.
And he was a bit of a Nazi.
He had a young son who took a fancy to me and I to him.
By that time, I was 13-- 12, 13, 14.
And I remember that we had to hide in the elevator up and down to-- to steal a kiss here and there.
[LAUGHTER] But he wasn't allowed to be seen with me, because I was Jewish and he wasn't.
That was back in Budapest, I would say in '40, '41.
My word.
Very much so.
Of course, as I told you before, the laws came in every six months, something new that the Jews are not allowed to do that.
The Jewish children are not allowed to go to the-- certain schools, because they don't need education or whatever.
So they-- we had quite a lot of restrictions by that time.
In the beginning, I don't remember them.
Yes, I did.
And that was before the Hungarian war.
And it was called Omike-- O-M-I-K-E.

It was a Jewish organization of-- I don't know what the ones in Sydney are, in Australia, Betar-- where they got together on Sunday for a dance, or for going out into the countryside, or having a picnic, or things like that.
And I remember I had-- by that time, I was 14, 14, 15.
And I remember.

I had a boyfriend who-- of course, boyfriend in those days was not going to bed with a guy, but to go out, that he came up to your home and asked your mother's permission to take you out to a movie, or to a dance, or to a meal, or something like that.
So that was my boyfriend.
He was a pianist, a very good one on top of it.
And he was our group's pianist when we had a, when we had a dancing group.
And of course, I always missed out to dance with him.
So he got a radiogram-- is that what they called it?
A phonogram.
Phonogram, and put on a few records, and then he just grabbed me.
And we went dancing.
So these were little memory.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Yes.
Klein-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [?
Lesley ?] Klein.
Yes, he did not survive.
Yeah, I know.
Yeah, but that-- those were, again, memories which I already aware of, very much so.
And I remember, I brought home a little puppy whom I found on the street.
And, and my grandmother throw me out with, with the dog together, sort of.
We can't afford to have a dog in the, in the apartment.
Yes.
And my mother was not around anymore.
She passed on.
So that's all--
What do you call war then?
The war started with me in '39.
In 1939, I was, as you know, a young kid of 12 years-- yeah.
And that was the last time I saw my father.
Because he was born originally in Vienna, in Austria.
And he was not a Hungarian state person.

So when the Germans were tightening, or the Hungarian Nazis, the [NON-ENGLISH], were tightening the grip on the Hungarian Jews, he was one of them who wasn't allowed to stay in Hungary because he wasn't a Hungarian citizen.
So he tried to get a permit or a visa into several countries in Europe.
And Belgium was the only place where they let him in.
Now, he spoke six languages.
That meant that French was a fluent language for him as well.
And he had no fear to survive in Brussels, in Belgium.
And he, he played a beautiful jazz piano and-- not classical as much.
But he did play classical as well.
And he went as a pianist into Belgium, to Brussels.
And he lived there for-- from '39 till '42.
And he always tried to, to send some money home to Budapest.
My mother was working as a secretary, clerical secretary.
And my brother and I went to school.
By that time, it got-- we got back to Budapest, that was after the boarding schools.
As well, by then in a public school, which was very close to us.
It was just a normal public school where Jews were there and non-Jews.
And I lost the thread.
With Grandma and my auntie.
Maternal-- my paternal stayed in Bratislava.
And my maternal was the one where my brother, Fredi, and my mother came-- my father, for all I know-- to Hungary from Wunstorf, right?
And I stayed with Grandma-- yeah, the paternal grandma.
So we stayed in, in Budapest with my maternal relatives, with my mother.
My father wasn't there anymore, right?
He went to Brussels.
And we went to school until I was 14.
In the meantime, my auntie gave birth to a daughter, Vera, whom I adored.
I, I really always, my ambition was to get-- to become a mother, to push the pram of a baby.
I somehow felt I had so much affection and love to give to someone that that little baby in the pram was the only one whom I can give it to.
Because my mother was a very sick person.
She had angina pectoris, which meant that she had a problem with the heart-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how you say that in English.
A very weak heart-- as a child she had scarlet fever.
And that was left over from a weak heart.
And of course, the knowledge that her husband wasn't with her and that it didn't look very kosher in those days, the Hungarian situation with the Jews.
She always was afraid that she won't see him anymore.
And she went down the hill emotionally.
And with emotional came the sickness.
And she died a natural-- well, natural death-- in 1943.
Yes.
So she, she lived to see her sister's baby, Vera, whom she loved very much.
And after that, as I said, she died.
And I stayed with my brother at the family's premises in the apartment.
